```"}],"urls":[{"text":"https://github.com/quarqlabs/agent-oss"},{"text":"https://github.com/quarqlabs/agent-oss"},{"text":"https://github.com/quarqlabs/agent-oss"}]},"plain_text":"For the past month, we had been focused on a goal: building an AI agent that supports actual continual learning.
Most useful assistants whether for work, research, or personal productivity need context that accumulates over time. They need to remember decisions, preferences and information gathered across dozens of conversations.
We quickly discovered that building this kind of memory is much harder than storing chat history.
An agent needs to retrieve the right information at the right moment, reason across events that happened weeks apart and avoid confidently inventing details when information is missing.
Over the last few weeks, we've been iterating on these problems inside Quarq Agent.
Today we're open-sourcing v0.4.0 and sharing both the system and the lessons we learned while building it.
GitHub: https://github.com/quarqlabs/agent-oss
This is our attempt to answer a simple question:
What would it take for an AI agent to actually remember?
What Makes Memory Difficult?
At first glance, memory seems like a storage problem. Save information somewhere, retrieve it when needed, and let the model generate an answer.
In practice, storage is the easiest part of the system.
The real challenges begin after information has already been recorded.
When a user asks a question, the agent must determine which memories are relevant, which version of a fact should be trusted, whether multiple pieces of information need to be combined, and how to handle situations where the available evidence is incomplete or contradictory.
Even seemingly simple questions can become surprisingly difficult when so many complications enter the picture.
Humans navigate these ambiguities almost automatically because memory is more than a collection of stored facts. 
We constantly evaluate and connect related information without consciously thinking about it.
AI systems don't have that luxury.
Building Quarq forced us to stop thinking about memory as a database problem. The architecture that emerged is ultimately a system for doing exactly that.
The Architecture of a Memory-First Agent
Most memory agents are built around a simple pattern: store conversations, retrieve the nearest matches, and inject them into the prompt.
That approach works surprisingly well for straightforward recall, but it struggles when questions require temporal reasoning, entity tracking, numerical synthesis, or connecting evidence scattered across multiple conversations.
Quarq takes a different approach. Rather than treating memory as a single retrieval problem, the system is organized into four layers that mirror the major challenges of long-term memory.
Each layer exists because the previous layer alone is insufficient.
1. Query: Finding the Right Evidence
The first challenge of memory is retrieval. People rarely ask questions using the same language that was originally stored in memory. A user might ask:
"When was the last time I saw Alex?"
while the original memory says:
"Had dinner with Alex at a Japanese restaurant."
Humans immediately recognize the connection between those two statements. Search systems often don't.
To bridge that gap, Quarq expands each question into several search hypotheses before retrieval begins. 
Each hypothesis approaches the problem from a different angle. Some focus on people, others on actions, dates, locations, or specific entities mentioned in the question.
Retrieval Pipeline
 
Each query is then executed across both vector and keyword indexes. Vector search is useful for finding conceptually similar memories, even when the wording differs. 
Keyword search complements it by providing stronger recall for exact names, dates, product names, and other structured information.
The results are merged, deduplicated, and ranked before being passed to the next stage. This combination consistently performed better than either retrieval strategy on its own.
2. Storage: Organizing Memory by Type
Finding information is only half of the problem. The system also needs to understand what kind of information it is working with.
Many memory systems store everything in a single collection and leave the language model to figure out the difference between a preference, a life event, and an instruction. 
That may work for small memory stores, but it becomes increasingly difficult as the amount of information grows.
Quarq separates memory into three categories, each with a distinct purpose.
Memory Structure
 
Semantic memory stores durable facts about the user and their world. Things like preferences, possessions, relationships, and long-term attributes belong here.
Episodic memory stores events that happened at a particular point in time. Conversations, meetings, purchases, trips, and decisions are all examples of episodic memories.
Procedural memory stores behavioral instructions. These memories describe how the agent should behave rather than facts about the user. Formatting preferences, tone guidelines, and project-specific conventions all fall into this category.
Under the hood, semantic and episodic memories are stored locally as FAISS vector indices backed by JSON records, while procedural memories live in a lightweight JSON rule store.
Separating memory in this way allows retrieval and reasoning to remain context-aware. 
A question about personal history can prioritize episodic memories, while a question about preferences can focus on semantic memory without introducing unnecessary noise.
3. Reasoning: Preventing Hallucinated Recall
Retrieving relevant memories does not guarantee a correct answer.
Even when the right information is available, language models can still make mistakes during reasoning. 
They may confuse dates, mix together information from different entities, or confidently answer a question despite having incomplete evidence.
To reduce these failures, Quarq applies a set of explicit reasoning constraints before generating a response.
Reasoning Layer
 
One category of safeguards focuses on time. The system explicitly distinguishes between when an event happened and when that event was recorded. 
This prevents storage timestamps from accidentally becoming event dates during reasoning.
Another set of safeguards focuses on numbers. Before using a numerical value, the model must determine who or what that number belongs to and what it is actually measuring. This helps prevent quantities from being attached to the wrong person, object, or event.
Quarq also enforces entity isolation. Facts associated with one person should not automatically transfer to another person simply because they appear nearby in memory.
Finally, the system can recognize when the available evidence is insufficient. Instead of guessing, it performs another retrieval pass with more targeted search queries and then attempts the answer again.
The guiding principle is simple:
If the evidence is incomplete, search again before generating an answer.
4. Learning: Keeping Memory Accurate
Memory is not static.
People change jobs. Preferences evolve. Projects start and end. New experiences accumulate while old information becomes outdated.
A useful memory system needs a mechanism for keeping its knowledge up to date rather than endlessly appending new information.
For that reason, Quarq treats memory as something that can be maintained over time.
Learning Loop
 
After a response is delivered, a dedicated learning model reviews the interaction and determines whether memory should change. Depending on the situation, it may create a new memory, update an existing one, or remove information that is no longer accurate.
This process runs asynchronously in the background, which means users do not have to wait for memory extraction before receiving a response.
Over time, the memory store becomes more structured and more accurate. Instead of accumulating endless duplicates, information is consolidated into a cleaner representation of the user's history and preferences.
Design Principles
Throughout development we accumulated a set of principles that repeatedly proved correct. These are not aspirational goals. They are constraints reflected directly in the architecture.
Retrieve Broadly, Reason Narrowly: Recall failures are usually retrieval failures. The system should search aggressively, then reason conservatively over evidence.
Evidence Beats Confidence: A convincing answer is not necessarily a correct answer. When supporting evidence is missing, the system should acknowledge uncertainty rather than invent details.
Time Is a First-Class Concern: Memory systems frequently fail because timestamps, event dates, and relative references become mixed together. Temporal reasoning deserves dedicated safeguards.
Learning Should Not Increase Latency: Users should not pay a performance penalty every time memory improves. Learning belongs in the background whenever possible.
Context Is a Resource: Large context windows create the illusion of simplicity while introducing new retrieval and reasoning problems. Only relevant information should be loaded into the prompt.
Getting Started
One of our goals with Quarq was minimizing operational complexity.
The complete source code is available at: https://github.com/quarqlabs/agent-oss

To get started

Or launch the API server:
 
Memory is stored locally and automatically created on first use.
Current Capabilities
Quarq currently includes integrations for:
Gmail (send, draft, search, and read email)
Google Calendar scheduling and availability management
PDF generation and report creation
Runtime identity and personality management
New capabilities can be added by dropping a skill definition into the tools directory.
The agent automatically discovers and registers available skills at startup.
Closing Thoughts
Quarq Agent was originally built as an exploration of long-term memory systems for AI agents.
Over the course of past month, it became a useful testbed for ideas around retrieval, memory organization and asynchronous learning. Many of the concepts described in this post were developed and validated through that work.
As a company, however, our focus has shifted.
Today, QuarqLabs is building developer infrastructure for robotics, and that is where our future product development efforts are concentrated.
Rather than leaving the project private, we decided to open-source it as a reference implementation for anyone interested in memory-first agent architectures. We believe the ideas are still valuable, even if this repository is no longer part of our active product roadmap.
For that reason, Quarq Agent should be viewed as an archived research project rather than a production product. We'll keep the repository publicly available, but we do not currently plan to actively develop new features or provide ongoing maintenance.
If you're exploring memory systems, retrieval architectures, or long-term agent design, we hope the codebase serves as a useful resource.
GitHub: https://github.com/quarqlabs/agent-oss"}
